examples.explanation: Initial: visited={0}, flips=0. Iteration 1: DFS from 0: edges 0→1 (flip, flips=1), 4→0 (reverse, no flip). New visited={0,1,4}. Iteration 2: DFS from 0: edges 0→1 (already visited), 1→3 (flip, flips=2), 4→0 (reverse), 4→5 (flip, flips=3). New visited={0,1,3,4,5}. Iteration 3: DFS from 0: edges 0→1, 1→3, 2→3 (reverse from 3→2, but 2 not visited yet), 4→0, 4→5. All nodes visited except 2. From node 3 (reached via 1→3), rev[3] includes 2. Since 2 not visited, add via reverse edge (no flip). All nodes now visited. Total flips=3.

Initial: visited={0}, flips=0. Iteration 1: DFS from 0: edge 1→0 (reverse direction, already correct). graph[0] is empty, rev[0]=[1,2]. So from node 0, check rev[0]: neighbors 1 and 2 not visited, so add to stack and visited. No flips because no edge points away from 0. New visited={0,1,2}. All nodes visited. Total flips=0.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n^2)
approaches.summary: Simulate all possible edge reorderings by repeatedly performing DFS from node 0, flipping edges that point away from 0 until all nodes are reachable. This is inefficient as it may require many iterations over the graph.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Reorder Routes to Root
ladders.desc: Complete solution using brute force DFS iterations to flip edges until all nodes are reachable from node 0.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete implementation of the brute force O(n²) approach for the 'Reorder Routes to Root' problem. The intuition is that we can repeatedly perform DFS from node 0, flipping any edges that point away from node 0 (making them point toward node 0), until all nodes become reachable. This works because each iteration makes progress by correcting at least one misdirected edge, though it may require many iterations in worst-case scenarios. The algorithm: 1) Represent the graph as adjacency lists for both directions (original and reversed). 2) Initialize a visited set with node 0. 3) While there are unvisited nodes: a) Perform DFS from node 0 using current edge directions. b) For each edge encountered that points away from node 0 (i.e., from visited to unvisited node in original direction), flip it by incrementing a counter and updating the graph representation. c) Mark newly visited nodes. 4) Return the flip counter. Time complexity is O(n²) because in worst case (like a linear chain pointing away from 0), we need O(n) iterations each doing O(n) DFS work. Space is O(n) for adjacency lists and visited set. This connects to L1 by using the DFS subroutine repeatedly. Common mistakes include not handling both edge directions properly, infinite loops if flip logic is incorrect, and off-by-one errors in counting. Real code pattern: def minReorder(n, connections): graph = [[] for _ in range(n)], rev = [[] for _ in range(n)], for u,v in connections: graph[u].append(v), rev[v].append(u), flips = 0, visited = set([0]), while len(visited) < n: stack = [0], new_visited = set(visited), flips_this_iter = 0, while stack: node = stack.pop(), for nei in graph[node]: if nei not in visited: flips_this_iter += 1, for nei in rev[node]: if nei not in visited: stack.append(nei), new_visited.add(nei), flips += flips_this_iter, visited = new_visited, return flips.

examples.trace: Initial: visited={0}, flips=0. Iteration 1: DFS from 0: edges 0→1 (flip, flips=1), 4→0 (reverse, no flip). New visited={0,1,4}. Iteration 2: DFS from 0: edges 0→1 (already visited), 1→3 (flip, flips=2), 4→0 (reverse), 4→5 (flip, flips=3). New visited={0,1,3,4,5}. Iteration 3: DFS from 0: edges 0→1, 1→3, 2→3 (reverse from 3→2, but 2 not visited yet), 4→0, 4→5. All nodes visited except 2. From node 3 (reached via 1→3), rev[3] includes 2. Since 2 not visited, add via reverse edge (no flip). All nodes now visited. Total flips=3.

Initial: visited={0}, flips=0. Iteration 1: DFS from 0: edge 1→0 (reverse direction, already correct). graph[0] is empty, rev[0]=[1,2]. So from node 0, check rev[0]: neighbors 1 and 2 not visited, so add to stack and visited. No flips because no edge points away from 0. New visited={0,1,2}. All nodes visited. Total flips=0.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS with Edge Flipping
ladders.desc: Perform DFS from node 0 to identify and flip edges pointing away from visited nodes.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core of each iteration in the brute force approach. The intuition is to traverse the graph from node 0 using current edge directions, and whenever we encounter an edge that goes from a visited node to an unvisited node in the original direction, we flip it (count it as a reorder) because it prevents reachability. This ensures we make progress toward making all nodes reachable. The algorithm: 1) Input: graph adjacency lists (original and reversed), current visited set. 2) Initialize a stack with node 0, a new visited set copy, and a flip counter. 3) While stack not empty: pop node, for each neighbor in original graph[node]: if neighbor not in visited, increment flip counter (this edge needs flipping). For each neighbor in reversed graph[node]: if neighbor not in visited, push to stack and add to new visited. 4) Return flip count and new visited set. Time complexity is O(n) per DFS as we visit each node and edge once. Space is O(n) for stack and sets. This feeds into L0 by being called repeatedly until all nodes are visited. Common mistakes include not checking both directions, incorrectly counting flips for edges that are already correct, and not updating visited properly. Real code pattern: def dfs_flip(graph, rev, visited): stack=[0], new_visited=set(visited), flips=0, while stack: node=stack.pop(), for nei in graph[node]: if nei not in visited: flips+=1, for nei in rev[node]: if nei not in visited: stack.append(nei), new_visited.add(nei), return flips, new_visited

examples.trace: Start: stack=[0], new_visited={0}, flips=0. Pop 0: graph[0]=[1] → flips=1. rev[0]=[4] → add 4. Pop 4: graph[4]=[0,5] → nei=5 not visited, flips=2. Return flips=2, new_visited={0,1,4}.

Start: stack=[0], new_visited={0}, flips=0. Pop 0: graph[0]=[], no flips. rev[0]=[1,2], nei=1 not in visited, push 1, add to new_visited; nei=2 not in visited, push 2, add to new_visited. Stack=[1,2]. Pop 2: graph[2]=[0], nei=0 in visited, no flip. rev[2]=[], no push. Pop 1: graph[1]=[0], nei=0 in visited, no flip. rev[1]=[], no push. Stack empty. Return flips=0, new_visited={0,1,2}.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Identifying and Flipping Misdirected Edges
ladders.desc: Determine which edges point away from the current visited set and need reordering.
ladders.explanation: This is the central insight of the brute force approach: to make all nodes reachable from node 0, we need to flip edges that are directed away from the currently reachable nodes (visited set) toward unreachable nodes. The intuition is that during DFS, if we traverse an edge in its original direction from a visited node to an unvisited node, that edge is misdirected because it should point toward node 0 to allow reachability; flipping it corrects this. Conversely, edges in reverse direction from unvisited to visited are already correct or can be traversed without flipping. The algorithm: Given a graph with original directions (graph) and reverse directions (rev), and a visited set, iterate through nodes in visited. For each node, check its outgoing edges in graph: if the neighbor is not in visited, count it as a flip. Also, check incoming edges via rev to find new nodes to visit. This logic is applied during DFS to efficiently traverse. Time complexity for this logic per edge check is O(1). Space is O(1) extra. This feeds into L1 by being the condition used in DFS. Common mistakes include flipping edges that are already correct (e.g., from visited to visited), or missing edges that need flipping because they're not directly from visited nodes. Real code pattern: for node in visited: for nei in graph[node]: if nei not in visited: flips += 1.

examples.trace: For each node in visited: Node 0: graph[0]=[1], nei=1 is in visited, no flip. Node 1: graph[1]=[3], nei=3 not in visited, flip count=1. Node 4: graph[4]=[0,5], nei=0 in visited, nei=5 not in visited, flip count=2. Total flips=2.
Node 0: graph[0]=[], no edges. No flips.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation with Reverse Edges
ladders.desc: Construct adjacency lists for both original and reverse directions from edge list.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is essential for efficiently traversing the graph in both directions during DFS. The intuition is that to check edges pointing away from visited nodes (original direction) and to find new nodes via edges pointing toward visited nodes (reverse direction), we need quick access to both outgoing and incoming edges for each node. By building two adjacency lists—one for original edges and one for reversed edges—we can perform O(1) lookups during traversal. The algorithm: 1) Initialize two lists: graph of size n for original directions, rev of size n for reverse directions. 2) For each edge [u, v] in connections: add v to graph[u] (original direction u→v), and add u to rev[v] (reverse direction v→u). 3) Return graph and rev. Time complexity is O(m) where m is number of edges. Space is O(n + m) for the lists. This feeds into L2 by providing the data structures needed to identify misdirected edges. Common mistakes include not initializing lists properly, confusing directions, or missing nodes with no edges. Real code pattern: n = number of nodes, graph = [[] for _ in range(n)], rev = [[] for _ in range(n)], for u,v in connections: graph[u].append(v), rev[v].append(u).

examples.trace: Initialize graph and rev as lists of size 3. Edge [0,1]: add 1 to graph[0], add 0 to rev[1]. Edge [1,2]: add 2 to graph[1], add 1 to rev[2]. Result: graph[0]=[1], graph[1]=[2], graph[2]=[]; rev[0]=[], rev[1]=[0], rev[2]=[1].
Initialize for n=2. Edge [1,0]: add 0 to graph[1], add 1 to rev[0]. So graph[0]=[], graph[1]=[0]; rev[0]=[1], rev[1]=[].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Over Adjacency Lists
ladders.desc: Traverse neighbors of a node in a graph represented as adjacency list.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves accessing and processing all neighbors of a given node in a graph. The intuition is that in graph algorithms, we often need to explore connections from a node to its adjacent nodes, which is efficiently done using an adjacency list (a list of lists where each index corresponds to a node and its list contains neighboring nodes). The algorithm: Given a graph adjacency list and a node index, use a loop to iterate through graph[node] and perform some action on each neighbor (e.g., check if visited, count flips, etc.). Time complexity is O(degree of node) per iteration. Space is O(1) extra. This feeds into L3 by being used when building or traversing the graph. Common mistakes include index out of bounds, modifying the list while iterating, or missing neighbors due to incorrect data structure. Real code pattern: for neighbor in graph[node]: # do something with neighbor.

examples.trace: graph[0] is the list [1,2]. Iterate: first neighbor 1, then neighbor 2.
graph[1] is [0]. Iterate: neighbor 0.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph as Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Represent a graph using a list of lists where each index stores neighbors of a node.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: understanding how to represent a graph in code. A graph consists of nodes (vertices) and edges connecting them. An adjacency list is a common representation where we use an array or list of lists; each index i corresponds to node i, and the list at that index contains all nodes that are directly connected from node i by an edge. The intuition is that it provides efficient storage and traversal for sparse graphs. For example, in a directed graph with edges 0→1 and 0→2, the adjacency list would be: graph[0] = [1,2], graph[1] = [], graph[2] = []. To build it: initialize a list of empty lists for each node, then for each edge [u,v], append v to graph[u]. Time to build is O(m) where m is edges. Space is O(n + m). This feeds into L4 by providing the data structure for iterating over neighbors. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in node indices, not initializing enough lists, or confusing directed vs undirected edges. Real code pattern: n = number of nodes, graph = [[] for _ in range(n)], for u,v in edges: graph[u].append(v).

examples.trace: Initialize graph as list of 3 empty lists: [[],[],[]]. For edge [0,1]: append 1 to graph[0], so graph becomes [[1],[],[]]. For edge [0,2]: append 2 to graph[0], so graph becomes [[1,2],[],[]].
Initialize graph as [[],[]]. For edge [1,0]: append 0 to graph[1], so graph becomes [[],[0]].

approaches.name: DFS with Direction Tracking O(n)
approaches.summary: Perform a DFS from node 0, treating the graph as undirected to traverse all nodes, but count edges that point away from 0 as needing reordering. This efficiently determines the minimum reorders by tracking edge directions during traversal.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Reorder Routes to Root using DFS with Direction Tracking
ladders.desc: Complete solution to determine minimum edge reversals needed so all nodes can reach node 0 in a directed tree.
ladders.explanation: The problem requires finding the minimum number of edge direction changes in a directed tree (n nodes labeled 0 to n-1) so that every node can reach node 0. The graph has n-1 edges forming a tree (connected, no cycles). The key insight is that we can treat the tree as undirected to traverse all nodes from node 0 using DFS/BFS, while keeping track of original edge directions. During traversal from parent to child, if the original edge points from child to parent (away from 0), it needs to be reversed. We count such edges. Intuition: Starting DFS from root (0), we explore all nodes. For each edge traversed from current node u to neighbor v, if the original directed edge exists from u to v, it's already pointing toward 0 (since we're moving from parent to child in DFS, and 0 is root, edges toward 0 should point from child to parent). In DFS from 0, when we go from parent u to child v, if the original directed edge is u→v, it points away from 0 and needs reversal. In the desired tree, all edges must point toward root 0. In the desired state, all edges should point towards 0 (i.e., each node should have a directed path to 0). That means for any edge between parent p and child c (where p is closer to 0), the edge should be c -> p. So if original edge is p -> c, it points away from 0 and needs reversal. In DFS from 0, we traverse from parent to child. So when we go from p to c, we check if original directed edge exists from p to c. If yes, it's pointing away from 0, so count 1. If original directed edge exists from c to p, it's already pointing toward 0, so no count. Algorithm: 1. Build adjacency list for undirected graph, but also store direction information. For each edge [a,b] in connections, add b to adj[a] with direction 1 (meaning original edge a->b), and add a to adj[b] with direction 0 (meaning original edge b->a does not exist, but we add for undirected traversal). store pair (neighbor, direction) where direction=1 if original edge is from current to neighbor, else 0. 2. DFS from node 0 with parent to avoid cycles. For each neighbor v of u (if v != parent): if direction == 1, increment count (needs reversal) and recurse on v. 3. Return count. Time O(n) since we visit each node once, each edge twice (undirected). Space O(n) for adjacency list and recursion stack. Connection to L1: This uses the DFS traversal with direction check as the key subroutine. Common mistakes: Not handling undirected traversal correctly, missing direction tracking, incorrect direction logic. Real code pattern: def minReorder(n, connections): adj = [[] for _ in range(n)] for a,b in connections: adj[a].append((b,1)) adj[b].append((a,0)) def dfs(u, parent): count=0 for v,dir in adj[u]: if v==parent: continue if dir==1: count+=1 count+=dfs(v,u) return count return dfs(0,-1)

examples.trace: Build adjacency: 0: [(1,1),(4,0)], 1: [(0,0),(3,1)], 2: [(3,1)], 3: [(1,0),(2,0)], 4: [(0,1),(5,1)], 5: [(4,0)]. DFS from 0, parent=-1. u=0: neighbors (1,1): dir=1 -> count=1, recurse dfs(1,0); (4,0): dir=0 -> no count, recurse dfs(4,0). dfs(1,0): neighbors (0,0): skip parent; (3,1): dir=1 -> count=1, recurse dfs(3,1). dfs(3,1): neighbors (1,0): skip parent; (2,0): dir=0 -> no count, recurse dfs(2,3). dfs(2,3): neighbors (3,1): skip parent; no other neighbors, return 0. So dfs(3,1) returns 0. dfs(1,0) returns 1+0=1. dfs(4,0): neighbors (0,1): skip parent; (5,1): dir=1 -> count=1, recurse dfs(5,4). dfs(5,4): neighbors (4,0): skip parent; return 0. dfs(4,0) returns 1+0=1. Total count = 1 (from edge 0->1) + 1 (from edge 1->3) + 1 (from edge 4->5) = 3.

Adjacency: 0: [(1,0)], 1: [(0,1),(2,1)], 2: [(1,0),(3,0)], 3: [(2,1),(4,1)], 4: [(3,0)]. DFS from 0, parent=-1. u=0: neighbors (1,0): dir=0 -> no count, recurse dfs(1,0). dfs(1,0): neighbors (0,1): skip parent; (2,1): dir=1 -> count=1, recurse dfs(2,1). dfs(2,1): neighbors (1,0): skip parent; (3,0): dir=0 -> no count, recurse dfs(3,2). dfs(3,2): neighbors (2,1): skip parent; (4,1): dir=1 -> count=1, recurse dfs(4,3). dfs(4,3): neighbors (3,0): skip parent; return 0. dfs(3,2) returns 1. dfs(2,1) returns 0+1=1. dfs(1,0) returns 1+1=2. Total count=2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Traversal with Direction Check
ladders.desc: DFS function that traverses an undirected tree while counting edges pointing away from root based on original directions.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine performs a depth-first search (DFS) on an undirected tree starting from a given node (root), using a parent parameter to avoid cycles. For each edge traversed from current node u to neighbor v, it checks if the original directed edge (from the input) exists from u to v. If yes, it increments a counter because that edge points away from the root and needs reversal. The intuition: In a tree rooted at 0, all edges should point towards the root for every node to reach 0. During DFS from root, we move from parent to child. If the original edge is from parent to child, it's oriented away from root, so counting it gives the number of reversals needed. The algorithm: function dfs(u, parent): count = 0; for each (v, direction) in adjacency list of u: if v == parent: continue; if direction == 1: count += 1; count += dfs(v, u); return count. Direction is 1 if original edge u->v exists, else 0. Time O(n) as each node visited once. Space O(n) for recursion stack. Connection to L0: This is the core DFS called from main function. Common mistakes: Forgetting parent check causing infinite recursion, misinterpreting direction. Real code pattern: def dfs(node, parent, adj): total=0; for nei,dir in adj[node]: if nei==parent: continue; if dir==1: total+=1; total+=dfs(nei,node,adj); return total.

examples.trace: dfs(0,-1): neighbors (1,1): dir=1 -> count=1, recurse dfs(1,0); (2,0): dir=0 -> no count, recurse dfs(2,0). dfs(1,0): neighbors (0,0): skip parent; (3,1): dir=1 -> count=1, recurse dfs(3,1). dfs(3,1): neighbors (1,0): skip parent; return 0. So dfs(1,0) returns 1+0=1. dfs(2,0): neighbors (0,1): skip parent; (4,0): dir=0 -> no count, recurse dfs(4,2). dfs(4,2): neighbors (2,1): skip parent; return 0. dfs(2,0) returns 0. Total count = 2 (edges 0→1 and 1→3 need reversal).

dfs(0,-1): neighbors (1,0): dir=0 -> no count, recurse dfs(1,0). dfs(1,0): neighbors (0,1): skip parent; (2,1): dir=1 -> count=1, recurse dfs(2,1). dfs(2,1): neighbors (1,0): skip parent; (3,0): dir=0 -> no count, recurse dfs(3,2). dfs(3,2): neighbors (2,1): skip parent; return 0. dfs(2,1) returns 0. dfs(1,0) returns 1+0=1. Total count=1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Counting Edges Pointing Away from Root in Tree Traversal
ladders.desc: The central technique of incrementing a counter when traversing an edge that originally points away from the root.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves, during a tree traversal (DFS or BFS), for each edge moved from current node u to neighbor v, checking if the original directed edge is from u to v. If yes, increment a reversal counter. Intuition: In a tree, we root at node 0. For every edge between parent p and child c (where p is closer to 0), the desired direction is from child to parent (c -> p) so that child can reach parent and eventually 0. During traversal from root outward, we visit parent before child. When we traverse edge from p to c, if original edge is p -> c, it's opposite to desired, so count 1. If original is c -> p, it's already correct, count 0. This works because traversal ensures we see each edge exactly once in the direction from root to leaves. Algorithm: While traversing, for each step u -> v: if edge u->v exists in original graph: count++. This is applied recursively. Time O(1) per edge. Space O(1) extra. Connection to L1: This is the condition check inside DFS. Common mistakes: Confusing direction, not accounting for undirected traversal. Real code pattern: if direction_from_u_to_v == 1: count += 1.

examples.input: Traversal step: from u=0 to v=1, original edge exists 0->1
examples.trace: At node 0, moving to neighbor 1. Check if original directed edge 0->1 exists. Yes, so increment count by 1.

examples.input: Traversal step: from u=1 to v=2, original edge exists 2->1
examples.trace: At node 1, moving to neighbor 2. Check if original directed edge 1->2 exists. No (original is 2->1), so no increment.

testCases.input: u=3, v=4, no original edge between them

ladders.title: Building Block — Constructing Undirected Adjacency List with Direction Flags
ladders.desc: Create an adjacency list for undirected graph from directed edges, storing flags indicating original direction.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves converting a list of directed edges into an adjacency list representation for an undirected graph, where each entry includes a neighbor and a direction flag. The flag indicates whether the original directed edge points from the current node to the neighbor. Intuition: To traverse the tree as undirected from root 0, we need to know all neighbors regardless of direction, but during traversal we must check original directions to count reversals. For each directed edge [a,b] in input, we add b to adj[a] with flag=1 (meaning edge a->b exists), and add a to adj[b] with flag=0 (meaning edge b->a does not exist originally, but we add for undirected connectivity). This ensures we can traverse both ways. Algorithm: Initialize adj as list of empty lists size n. For each edge (a,b) in connections: adj[a].append((b,1)); adj[b].append((a,0)). Time O(n) to process n-1 edges. Space O(n) for adjacency list. Connection to L2: This provides the data structure for traversal and direction checks. Common mistakes: Incorrect flag assignment, not adding both directions. Real code pattern: adj = [[] for _ in range(n)]; for a,b in connections: adj[a].append((b,1)); adj[b].append((a,0)).

examples.trace: For edge [0,1]: add (1,1) to adj[0]; add (0,0) to adj[1]. For edge [1,2]: add (2,1) to adj[1]; add (1,0) to adj[2]. Result: adj[0]=[(1,1)], adj[1]=[(0,0),(2,1)], adj[2]=[(1,0)].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Adding Directed Edge to Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation of inserting a neighbor with direction flag into an adjacency list.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves adding an entry to an adjacency list for a graph. Specifically, given a node u, a neighbor v, and a direction flag d, we append the pair (v, d) to the list adj[u]. The direction flag d is 1 if the original directed edge is from u to v, and 0 otherwise. Intuition: Adjacency list is a common graph representation where each node has a list of its neighbors. By including a flag, we store additional information about edge direction for later use. This operation is performed for each directed edge in the input, twice per edge (once for each direction with appropriate flag). Algorithm: adj[u].append((v, d)). Time O(1) per addition. Space O(1) per edge. Connection to L3: This is repeated to build the full adjacency list. Common mistakes: Appending incorrectly, confusing flag values. Real code pattern: adj[a].append((b, 1)) for edge a->b.

examples.input: adj[0] initially empty, add neighbor 1 with direction 1
examples.trace: Start with adj[0] = []. After append((1,1)), adj[0] becomes [(1,1)].
Current adj[1] = [(0,0)]. Append (2,1) to get [(0,0),(2,1)].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Adjacency List Representation
ladders.desc: Representing a graph as a list of lists, where each index corresponds to a node and contains its neighbors.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept involves representing a graph using an adjacency list. For a graph with n nodes labeled 0 to n-1, we create a list 'adj' of size n, where each element adj[i] is a list of nodes that are directly connected to node i. Intuition: This data structure efficiently stores graph connectivity, allowing quick access to neighbors of any node. It's space-efficient for sparse graphs (like trees). For undirected graphs, if node i is connected to j, we add j to adj[i] and i to adj[j]. For directed graphs, we only add in the direction of the edge. In our problem, we use it for undirected traversal but with extra direction info. Algorithm: Initialize adj = [[] for _ in range(n)]. To add an undirected edge between i and j: adj[i].append(j); adj[j].append(i). Time O(1) per addition. Space O(n + e) where e is number of edges. Connection to L4: This is the base structure before adding direction flags. Common mistakes: Index out of range, not initializing properly. Real code pattern: n = number of nodes; adj = [[] for _ in range(n)].

examples.trace: Initialize adj = [[], [], []]. For edge [0,1]: add 1 to adj[0], add 0 to adj[1]. Now adj[0]=[1], adj[1]=[0]. For edge [1,2]: add 2 to adj[1], add 1 to adj[2]. Final: adj[0]=[1], adj[1]=[0,2], adj[2]=[1].

Start adj = [[],[],[],[]]. Edge [0,2]: adj[0].append(2), adj[2].append(0). Edge [1,3]: adj[1].append(3), adj[3].append(1). Edge [2,3]: adj[2].append(3), adj[3].append(2). Result: adj[0]=[2], adj[1]=[3], adj[2]=[0,3], adj[3]=[1,2].

approaches.name: BFS with Adjacency Lists O(n)
approaches.summary: Use BFS starting from node 0, building adjacency lists that separate outgoing and incoming edges, and increment a counter for edges that must be reversed to ensure connectivity. This approach leverages level-order traversal for clarity and efficiency.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Reorder Routes to Root using BFS with Adjacency Lists
ladders.desc: Complete solution counting edges to reverse so all nodes can reach node 0 via BFS traversal.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires reordering directed edges in a tree (n nodes, n-1 edges) so every node can reach node 0. The BFS approach starts from node 0 and traverses outward, counting edges that point away from node 0 (which need reversal). We build two adjacency lists: one for original outgoing edges (from parent to child) and one for incoming edges (from child to parent). Starting BFS from node 0, we process neighbors: if the original edge is outgoing from current (parent) to neighbor (child), it points away from 0 and needs reversal; if incoming (child → parent), it is already correct. (increment counter). We mark visited nodes to avoid cycles. Intuition: Node 0 is the root; edges should point toward it. BFS ensures we process nodes level by level, counting reversals as we encounter edges pointing away from the root. Time: O(n) for building adjacency lists and BFS traversal. Space: O(n) for adjacency lists and queue. Connection to L1: This integrates the BFS traversal with adjacency list processing. Common mistakes: Not separating outgoing/incoming edges, missing visited checks, or incorrect reversal logic. Real code pattern: Build adjacency lists, BFS from 0 with queue, count reversals when edge direction is from neighbor to current.

examples.trace: Step 1: Build adjacency lists. Outgoing: {0:[1], 1:[3], 2:[3], 4:[0,5]}. Incoming: {1:[0], 3:[1,2], 0:[4], 5:[4]}. Step 2: BFS from node 0. Queue=[0], visited={0}, count=0. Step 3: Process node 0. Neighbors: 1 (outgoing 0→1, needs reversal → count=1), 4 (incoming 4→0, already correct). Add 1,4 to queue. Queue=[1,4], visited={0,1,4}. Step 4: Process node 1. Neighbors: 3 (outgoing from 1, no reversal). Add 3 to queue. Queue=[4,3], visited={0,1,4,3}. Step 5: Process node 4. Neighbors: 0 (visited, skip), 5 (outgoing from 4, no reversal). Add 5 to queue. Queue=[3,5], visited={0,1,4,3,5}. Step 6: Process node 3. Neighbors: 1 (visited, skip), 2 (incoming from 2 to 3, reversal needed, count=2). Add 2 to queue. Queue=[5,2], visited={0,1,4,3,5,2}. Step 7: Process node 5. Neighbors: 4 (visited, skip). Queue=[2]. Step 8: Process node 2. Neighbors: 3 (visited, skip). Queue empty. Final count=3.

Step 1: Build adjacency lists. Outgoing: {1:[0,2], 3:[2,4]}. Incoming: {0:[1], 2:[1,3], 4:[3]}. Step 2: BFS from node 0. Queue=[0], visited={0}, count=0. Step 3: Process node 0. Neighbors: 1 (incoming from 1 to 0, reversal needed, count=1). Add 1 to queue. Queue=[1], visited={0,1}. Step 4: Process node 1. Neighbors: 0 (visited, skip), 2 (outgoing from 1, no reversal). Add 2 to queue. Queue=[2], visited={0,1,2}. Step 5: Process node 2. Neighbors: 1 (visited, skip), 3 (incoming from 3 to 2, reversal needed, count=2). Add 3 to queue. Queue=[3], visited={0,1,2,3}. Step 6: Process node 3. Neighbors: 2 (visited, skip), 4 (outgoing from 3, no reversal). Add 4 to queue. Queue=[4], visited={0,1,2,3,4}. Step 7: Process node 4. No unvisited neighbors. Queue empty. Final count=2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — BFS Traversal with Edge Direction Checking
ladders.desc: BFS implementation that processes nodes and checks edge directions to count reversals.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine performs BFS starting from a given node (node 0 in the full problem) and counts edges that need reversal. It uses pre-built adjacency lists for outgoing and incoming edges. The algorithm: initialize queue with start node, mark visited, set count=0. While queue not empty, pop current node. For each neighbor in combined neighbors from outgoing and incoming lists: if neighbor not visited, check edge direction. If edge is from current to neighbor (outgoing), it's correct; if from neighbor to current (incoming), it's incorrect and increment count. Then mark neighbor visited and enqueue. Intuition: BFS explores nodes level by level from the root; checking direction at each step ensures we count reversals as we move outward. Time: O(n) for visiting each node and edge once. Space: O(n) for queue and visited set. Connection to L0: This is the core traversal logic integrated into the full solution. Connection to L2: It relies on adjacency lists for direction checking. Common mistakes: Forgetting to check both outgoing and incoming lists, incorrect direction logic, or not marking visited before enqueueing. Real code pattern: Queue, visited set, while loop, neighbor iteration with if-else for direction check.

examples.trace: Step 1: Queue=[0], visited={0}, count=0. Step 2: Process node 0. Neighbors: 1 (outgoing 0→1, needs reversal). Add 1. Queue=[1], visited={0,1}. Step 3: Process node 1. Neighbors: 2 (outgoing 1→2, needs reversal). Add 2. Queue=[2], visited={0,1,2}. Step 4: Process node 2. No unvisited neighbors. Queue empty. Final count=0.

examples.input: start=0, outgoing={}, incoming={0:[1],1:[2]}, outgoing and incoming for others empty
examples.trace: Step 1: Queue=[0], visited={0}, count=0. Step 2: Process node 0. Neighbors: 1 (from incoming, incorrect direction, count=1). Add 1 to queue. Queue=[1], visited={0,1}. Step 3: Process node 1. Neighbors: 2 (from incoming, incorrect direction, count=2). Add 2 to queue. Queue=[2], visited={0,1,2}. Step 4: Process node 2. No unvisited neighbors. Queue empty. Final count=2.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Building Adjacency Lists for Outgoing and Incoming Edges
ladders.desc: Construct two adjacency lists from edge list to separate edge directions.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves creating two dictionaries (or lists): one for outgoing edges (from u to v) and one for incoming edges (from v to u). Given n nodes and a list of directed edges (connections), iterate through each edge [u,v]. Add v to outgoing[u] and add u to incoming[v]. This separation is crucial for the BFS traversal to quickly check edge directions. Intuition: In a directed tree, each edge has a direction; separating them allows efficient lookup during traversal to determine if an edge points toward or away from the root. Time: O(n) for iterating through n-1 edges. Space: O(n) for storing adjacency lists. Connection to L1: These lists are used in the BFS sub-routine for direction checking. Connection to L3: It relies on basic graph representation concepts. Common mistakes: Not initializing lists for all nodes, mixing up outgoing and incoming, or using inefficient data structures. Real code pattern: Initialize two lists of lists size n, loop through connections, append accordingly.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize outgoing=[[],[],[]], incoming=[[],[],[]]. Step 2: Process [0,1]: add 1 to outgoing[0]=[1]; add 0 to incoming[1]=[0]. Step 3: Process [1,2]: add 2 to outgoing[1]=[2]; add 1 to incoming[2]=[1]. Final: outgoing=[[1],[2],[]], incoming=[[],[0],[1]].

Step 1: Initialize outgoing=[[],[],[],[]], incoming=[[],[],[],[]]. Step 2: Process [2,3]: add 3 to outgoing[2]=[3]; add 2 to incoming[3]=[2]. Step 3: Process [1,2]: add 2 to outgoing[1]=[2]; add 1 to incoming[2]=[1]. Step 4: Process [0,1]: add 1 to outgoing[0]=[1]; add 0 to incoming[1]=[0]. Final: outgoing=[[1],[2],[3],[]], incoming=[[],[0],[1],[2]].

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation with Adjacency Lists
ladders.desc: Represent a directed graph using adjacency lists for efficient neighbor access.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves creating an adjacency list representation of a directed graph. An adjacency list is a data structure where each node has a list of its neighboring nodes. For a directed graph, if there's an edge from u to v, v is added to the list for u. This allows O(1) average time to access neighbors. Steps: initialize a list of lists of size n (number of nodes). For each edge [u,v], append v to the list at index u. Intuition: Adjacency lists are space-efficient for sparse graphs (like trees) and enable quick traversal algorithms like BFS. Time: O(n) to initialize and process edges. Space: O(n) for the lists. Connection to L2: This is extended to two lists for outgoing and incoming edges. Connection to L4: It uses list operations. Common mistakes: Using fixed-size arrays without dynamic lists, not handling node indices properly, or incorrect initialization. Real code pattern: adj = [[] for _ in range(n)]; for u,v in edges: adj[u].append(v).

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize adj=[[],[],[]]. Step 2: Process [0,1]: append 1 to adj[0]=[1]. Step 3: Process [1,2]: append 2 to adj[1]=[2]. Final adj=[[1],[2],[]].

Step 1: Initialize adj=[[],[],[],[]]. Step 2: Process [0,1]: append 1 to adj[0]=[1]. Step 3: Process [0,2]: append 2 to adj[0]=[1,2]. Step 4: Process [2,3]: append 3 to adj[2]=[3]. Final adj=[[1,2],[],[3],[]].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Through Edge List and Updating Lists
ladders.desc: Process a list of edges to build data structures by iterating and appending.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves looping through a list of pairs (edges) and performing updates on data structures (like lists). Given a list of edges where each edge is [u,v], we access u and v, then append v to a list associated with u. This requires basic iteration (for loop), list indexing, and list appending. Intuition: Many graph algorithms start by processing input edges to build internal representations; this operation is fundamental for that step. Time: O(e) where e is number of edges. Space: O(1) extra per iteration. Connection to L3: This operation is used to build adjacency lists. Connection to L5: It relies on control flow and list manipulation. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in indexing, not initializing lists properly, or inefficient appending. Real code pattern: for edge in edges: u,v = edge; adj[u].append(v).

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize adj=[[],[],[]]. Step 2: Process first edge [0,1]: u=0, v=1; append 1 to adj[0]=[1]. Step 3: Process second edge [1,2]: u=1, v=2; append 2 to adj[1]=[2]. Final adj=[[1],[2],[]].

Step 1: Initialize adj=[[],[],[],[]]. Step 2: Process first edge [2,3]: u=2, v=3; append 3 to adj[2]=[3]. Step 3: Process second edge [0,1]: u=0, v=1; append 1 to adj[0]=[1]. Final adj=[[1],[],[3],[]].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Lists and Basic Iteration in Programming
ladders.desc: Understand lists (arrays) and how to iterate through them with loops.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept covers lists (dynamic arrays) and iteration using loops. A list is an ordered collection of items, accessible by index starting from 0. Iteration is done with a for loop that goes through each item in the list. For example, to process a list of pairs, we use 'for edge in edges:' where edge is each pair. Basic operations include accessing elements (edge[0], edge[1]) and appending to lists (list.append(item)). Intuition: Lists store data sequentially; iteration allows processing each element, which is essential for handling input data in algorithms. Time: O(n) to iterate through n items. Space: O(1) for the loop variable. Connection to L4: This is used to iterate through edge lists. Common mistakes: Confusing list indices, not using proper loop syntax, or misunderstanding list mutability. Real code pattern: edges = [[0,1],[1,2]]; for edge in edges: u = edge[0]; v = edge[1]; print(u,v).

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize edges=[[0,1],[2,3]]. Step 2: Start for loop. First iteration: edge=[0,1]; access edge[0]=0, edge[1]=1; print or process (0,1). Step 3: Second iteration: edge=[2,3]; access edge[0]=2, edge[1]=3; process (2,3). Loop ends.
Step 1: Initialize edges=[[5,4],[3,2]]. Step 2: First iteration: edge=[5,4]; u=5, v=4; process (5,4). Step 3: Second iteration: edge=[3,2]; u=3, v=2; process (3,2). Loop ends.
